strengths: JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations applied consistently on all public API methods across QueryExecutor, ExplorationService, and handler layer
Sealed interface ExploreResponse with permits clause gives compile-time exhaustiveness for response variants (SingleSeed vs MultiSeed)
Records used for all immutable value objects (CollocateProfile, DiscoveredNoun, ExplorationOptions, etc.) with compact constructors enforcing Map.copyOf/List.copyOf
Optional<RelationConfig> used properly to model absence rather than null returns on GrammarConfig.relation()
Pattern matching instanceof used in GrammarConfigLoader and VisualizationHandlers instead of unsafe casts
type_safety.issue_character: A single intentional type-system weakening: the public SemanticFieldExplorer constructor accepts @Nullable GrammarConfig as a test escape hatch, creating a contract gap between the annotation and the production requirement stated only in prose.
type_safety.score_rationale: The type model is carefully designed: JSpecify annotations, sealed interfaces, records with defensive copies, and proper Optional usage are all consistently applied. The nullable GrammarConfig escape hatch on a public constructor is the main drag — it creates a documented but unchecked gap between the annotation system and the production requirement. This is a deliberate trade-off, but it still represents a type system weakening that callers cannot verify at compile time. The rest of the type model is solid.

issues.summary: Public SemanticFieldExplorer constructor accepts @Nullable GrammarConfig as test escape hatch, weakening production type contract
evidence: Constructor parameter annotated @Nullable GrammarConfig grammarConfig with Javadoc: 'null is accepted as a test-only escape hatch'
ExplorationHandlers enforces @NonNull GrammarConfig on its own constructor, creating inconsistent null semantics at adjacent seams
Tests invoke new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor, null) to bypass grammar config — exploits the public API weakening
issues.suggestion: Introduce a package-private or test-only factory (e.g. SemanticFieldExplorer.forTesting(executor)) rather than weakening the public constructor annotation. Keep the public constructor @NonNull.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer is an explicit thin facade: Javadoc says so, and the implementation confirms it — no algorithm logic, only delegation to SingleSeedExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator
ExploreResponseAssembler centralises all model-to-presentation translation; ExplorationHandlers never builds JSON directly
SeedCollocateData and AggregatedCollocateStats private records in MultiSeedExplorer crystallize intermediate data boundaries, preventing accumulator state from leaking across methods
SharedExploreParams record captures the common HTTP parameter group cleanly, avoiding repeated parseIntParam/parseDoubleParam calls at each handler
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Null semantics at adjacent seams are slightly inconsistent: ExplorationHandlers demands @NonNull GrammarConfig but SemanticFieldExplorer allows @Nullable in its public constructor, so the boundary rule is enforced on the API side but not on the service side.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The module-to-module handoffs are clean and explicit. The facade pattern is correctly thin, response assembly is centralised, and intermediate record types prevent accumulator leakage. The null-semantics inconsistency between ExplorationHandlers (@NonNull) and SemanticFieldExplorer (@Nullable) at adjacent seams is a minor roughness — it does not affect runtime correctness but creates a slightly uneven contract story across the HTTP-to-service boundary.

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers enforces @NonNull GrammarConfig but SemanticFieldExplorer's public constructor allows @Nullable, creating inconsistent seam contracts
evidence: ExplorationHandlers constructor: Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig, ...) — actively rejects null
SemanticFieldExplorer public constructor: @Nullable GrammarConfig grammarConfig — actively accepts null
Adjacent layers enforce opposite rules on the same value; a developer reading one seam gets misleading information about the other
issues.suggestion: Align null semantics: make SemanticFieldExplorer's public constructor @NonNull and introduce a package-private test constructor or factory for null-grammar use cases.

strengths: CollocateAccumulator private static final class in MultiSeedExplorer avoids four parallel maps and a second averaging pass — genuine single-pass elegance
Phase comments (Phase 1/2/3/4) and method extraction in SingleSeedExplorer make a 4-step algorithm scannable without a design doc
buildNounToCollocatesMap Javadoc documents the I/O fan-out concern and recommends a topPredicates ceiling — the right kind of inline warning
filterByMinShared is a direct, single-purpose method with no dead branches
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Small inefficiencies and style inconsistencies inside individual methods rather than any structural problem: wasted statistics computation, manual comparator chaining, and one inlined deduplication loop that would benefit from extraction.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The algorithmic code is cleanly phased and well-extracted. CollocateAccumulator is the highlight — it is exactly the right abstraction for the problem. The drag comes from minor craft issues: DoubleSummaryStatistics in buildDiscoveredNouns computes min/max/count when only avg and sum are used; identifyCoreCollocates manually chains comparisons rather than using Comparator composition. These are small but consistent enough to pull the score below solid.

issues.summary: buildDiscoveredNouns uses DoubleSummaryStatistics but only needs avg and sum; min/max/count computed and discarded
evidence: java.util.DoubleSummaryStatistics stats = sharedCollocates.values().stream().mapToDouble(Double::doubleValue).summaryStatistics()
Only stats.getAverage() and stats.getSum() are used; getMin(), getMax(), getCount() are never read
Could be replaced with two direct stream reductions or a simple loop accumulating sum and count
issues.suggestion: Replace with explicit sum/count accumulation: double sum = sharedCollocates.values().stream().mapToDouble(Double::doubleValue).sum(); double avg = count == 0 ? 0.0 : sum / count;

issues.summary: identifyCoreCollocates sorts with a manual two-level lambda rather than Comparator.comparingInt(...).thenComparingDouble(...)
evidence: coreCollocates.sort((a, b) -> { int cmp = Integer.compare(b.sharedByCount(), a.sharedByCount()); return cmp != 0 ? cmp : Double.compare(b.avgLogDice(), a.avgLogDice()); })
Comparator.comparingInt(CoreCollocate::sharedByCount).reversed().thenComparingDouble(CoreCollocate::avgLogDice).reversed() is more readable and consistent with Java Comparator idioms used elsewhere
issues.suggestion: Use Comparator.comparingInt(CoreCollocate::sharedByCount).reversed().thenComparingDouble(CoreCollocate::avgLogDice).reversed() to match standard Java comparator composition style.

strengths: CORE_COLLOCATE_MIN_NOUN_FRACTION constant explains its value semantics (1/3 ensures broad sharing, not just one or two nouns) — comments explain WHY
buildNounToCollocatesMap Javadoc flags the I/O fan-out risk and gives a concrete budget recommendation — adds real operational insight
ExplorationResult class Javadoc explains why it is a class rather than a record with reasoned trade-offs — design rationale, not padding
No entry/exit logging on trivial getter methods; no null checks on values that cannot be null in core algorithm paths
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: Entry-state debug logging inside loops in CollocateProfileComparator has the characteristic AI pattern of logging both the input and per-item details at the same level; combined with minor per-delegation Javadocs in SingleSeedExplorationOptions that restate the accessor name, the debt is localised to two subsystems.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: Most of the codebase avoids restating comments and over-documentation. The drag is concentrated in CollocateProfileComparator: 'Nouns: {}' and 'Min logDice: {}' log the method inputs, then per-noun adjective lists are dumped (subList(0,5)) inside the loop — this is the classic AI-pattern of logging everything on entry. SingleSeedExplorationOptions' delegation accessors have one-line Javadocs ('maximum collocates to retrieve per seed') that largely restate the accessor name. Together these push the score meaningfully below solid.

issues.summary: CollocateProfileComparator logs method inputs and per-item object dumps at debug level, a hallmark AI entry-logging pattern
evidence: logger.debug("Nouns: {}", seedNouns) and logger.debug("Min logDice: {}", minLogDice) at top of compareCollocateProfiles — logs inputs that are visible at the call site
logger.debug("Profiling {}: {} adjectives (top: {})", noun, adjectives.size(), adjectives.subList(0, Math.min(5, adjectives.size()))) inside the per-noun loop — object dump at debug level
debugLogTopProfiles iterates profiles at logger.debug level logging collocate detail per item in a loop
issues.suggestion: Collapse to one meaningful progress log per method call (e.g. 'Comparing %d nouns, top=%d, minLogDice=%.1f'). Remove per-item object dumps from inside loops; these belong in trace-level REPL instrumentation, not debug logs.

issues.summary: SingleSeedExplorationOptions delegation accessors have one-line Javadocs that restate the accessor name without adding insight
evidence: /** @return maximum collocates to retrieve per seed. */ public int topCollocates() { return base.topCollocates(); }
/** @return minimum logDice threshold for collocate inclusion. */ public double logDiceThreshold() { return base.logDiceThreshold(); }
Each Javadoc says nothing a developer cannot infer from the method name; the delegation bodies already make the purpose clear
issues.suggestion: Remove the Javadocs on these three delegation accessors entirely, or replace with a single class-level note that all three delegate to base() for call-site convenience.

strengths: StubQueryExecutor used consistently as the test isolation boundary — no real index needed for any unit test
ExplorationHandlersTest has 16+ tests covering both happy-path and negative/validation paths (missing params, one-seed comparison, unknown relation, unsupported param)
collocatingExecutor pattern in ExplorationHandlersTest provides real semantic data to verify actual content ('important' is in collocates), not just HTTP status codes
MultiSeedExplorerTest covers edge cases: empty executor, no shared collocates, seed ordering
SemanticFieldExplorerTest tests semantically meaningful scenarios: fully-shared, partially-shared, specific adjectives across three seeds
test_strategy.issue_character: Two quality defects in ExploreResponseAssemblerTest are isolated to one test class: three tautological size-assertions that can never fail, and a duplicate assertion copied within the same test method.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The test strategy is solid at the exploration and handler layers — content assertions, semantic fixtures, and negative-path coverage are all present and meaningful. The ExploreResponseAssemblerTest drags the score down: three assertTrue(list.size() >= 0) assertions (lines 88, 90, 92) are tautologically true and provide zero coverage guarantee, while the duplicate assertEquals at lines 104-105 is a copy-paste error. These are not severe but indicate that the assembler test was written carelessly relative to the rest of the suite.

issues.summary: Three assertTrue(list.size() >= 0) assertions in ExploreResponseAssemblerTest are always true and test nothing
evidence: assertTrue(response.seedCollocates().size() >= 0, "should have seed_collocates_count") — List.size() is always >= 0
assertTrue(response.primarySeeds().size() >= 0, "should have discovered_nouns_count") — same issue
assertTrue(response.coreCollocates().size() >= 0, "should have core_collocates_count") — same issue
The intent was to assert the key exists, but the assertion condition is vacuously true and never catches a regression
issues.suggestion: Replace each with assertNotNull(response.seedCollocates()) or assertEquals(expectedCount, response.seedCollocates().size()) using a known fixture count. The test already provides a fixture with 1 collocate entry, so assertEquals(1, response.seedCollocates().size()) is appropriate.

issues.summary: assertEquals(2, response.seedCollocates().size()) appears twice consecutively in populateExploreResponse_countsMatchListSizes
evidence: Line 104: assertEquals(2, response.seedCollocates().size());
Line 105: assertEquals(2, response.seedCollocates().size()); // exact duplicate
The second assertion was likely meant to check a different count (e.g. seedCollocatesCount field) but instead repeats the list size check
issues.suggestion: Remove the duplicate or replace one instance with an assertion on the count companion field if the response record exposes one.

findings.summary: Public SemanticFieldExplorer constructor accepts @Nullable GrammarConfig as test escape hatch, weakening production type contract
evidence: Constructor parameter annotated @Nullable GrammarConfig grammarConfig with Javadoc: 'null is accepted as a test-only escape hatch'
ExplorationHandlers enforces @NonNull GrammarConfig on its own constructor, creating inconsistent null semantics at adjacent seams
Tests invoke new SemanticFieldExplorer(executor, null) to bypass grammar config — exploits the public API weakening
findings.suggestion: Introduce a package-private or test-only factory (e.g. SemanticFieldExplorer.forTesting(executor)) rather than weakening the public constructor annotation. Keep the public constructor @NonNull.

evidence: ExplorationHandlers constructor: Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig, ...) — actively rejects null
SemanticFieldExplorer public constructor: @Nullable GrammarConfig grammarConfig — actively accepts null
Adjacent layers enforce opposite rules on the same value; a developer reading one seam gets misleading information about the other
findings.suggestion: Align null semantics: make SemanticFieldExplorer's public constructor @NonNull and introduce a package-private test constructor or factory for null-grammar use cases.

findings.summary: buildDiscoveredNouns uses DoubleSummaryStatistics but only needs avg and sum; min/max/count computed and discarded
evidence: java.util.DoubleSummaryStatistics stats = sharedCollocates.values().stream().mapToDouble(Double::doubleValue).summaryStatistics()
Only stats.getAverage() and stats.getSum() are used; getMin(), getMax(), getCount() are never read
Could be replaced with two direct stream reductions or a simple loop accumulating sum and count
findings.suggestion: Replace with explicit sum/count accumulation: double sum = sharedCollocates.values().stream().mapToDouble(Double::doubleValue).sum(); double avg = count == 0 ? 0.0 : sum / count;

findings.summary: identifyCoreCollocates sorts with a manual two-level lambda rather than Comparator.comparingInt(...).thenComparingDouble(...)
evidence: coreCollocates.sort((a, b) -> { int cmp = Integer.compare(b.sharedByCount(), a.sharedByCount()); return cmp != 0 ? cmp : Double.compare(b.avgLogDice(), a.avgLogDice()); })
Standard Java Comparator composition (comparingInt.reversed().thenComparingDouble.reversed()) is more readable and consistent
findings.suggestion: Use Comparator.comparingInt(CoreCollocate::sharedByCount).reversed().thenComparingDouble(CoreCollocate::avgLogDice).reversed() to match standard Java comparator composition style.

findings.summary: CollocateProfileComparator logs method inputs and per-item object dumps at debug level, a hallmark AI entry-logging pattern
evidence: logger.debug("Nouns: {}", seedNouns) and logger.debug("Min logDice: {}", minLogDice) at top of compareCollocateProfiles — logs inputs visible at the call site
logger.debug("Profiling {}: {} adjectives (top: {})", noun, adjectives.size(), adjectives.subList(0, Math.min(5, adjectives.size()))) inside per-noun loop — object dump at debug level
debugLogTopProfiles iterates profiles emitting per-item debug lines — full object dump loop
findings.suggestion: Collapse to one meaningful progress log per method call. Remove per-item object dumps from inside loops; move to trace level if needed for diagnostics.

findings.summary: SingleSeedExplorationOptions delegation accessors have one-line Javadocs that restate the accessor name without adding insight
evidence: /** @return maximum collocates to retrieve per seed. */ public int topCollocates() { return base.topCollocates(); }
/** @return minimum logDice threshold for collocate inclusion. */ public double logDiceThreshold() { return base.logDiceThreshold(); }
Each Javadoc says nothing a developer cannot infer from the method name; delegation is already apparent from the body
findings.suggestion: Remove the Javadocs on these three delegation accessors, or replace with a single class-level note that they delegate to base() for call-site convenience.

findings.summary: Three assertTrue(list.size() >= 0) assertions in ExploreResponseAssemblerTest are always true and test nothing
evidence: assertTrue(response.seedCollocates().size() >= 0, "should have seed_collocates_count") — List.size() is always >= 0
assertTrue(response.primarySeeds().size() >= 0, "should have discovered_nouns_count") — same tautology
assertTrue(response.coreCollocates().size() >= 0, "should have core_collocates_count") — same tautology
The fixture provides 1 seedCollocate, 1 discoveredNoun, 1 coreCollocate — asserting specific counts would catch real regressions
findings.suggestion: Replace with assertEquals(1, response.seedCollocates().size()), assertEquals(1, response.primarySeeds().size()), assertEquals(1, response.coreCollocates().size()) matching the provided fixture values.

findings.summary: assertEquals(2, response.seedCollocates().size()) appears twice consecutively in populateExploreResponse_countsMatchListSizes
evidence: Line 104: assertEquals(2, response.seedCollocates().size());
Line 105: assertEquals(2, response.seedCollocates().size()); // exact duplicate
The second assertion was likely meant to check a different property (e.g. seedCollocatesCount if the record exposed one) but instead repeats the same check
findings.suggestion: Remove the duplicate assertion. If a count companion field exists on the response, assert it separately: assertEquals(response.seedCollocates().size(), response.seedCollocatesCount()).
